coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance dual-model immunoassay based on 2,3-diaminophenazine and carbon dots for detecting Aflatoxin B1
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Die
Zhang, Junxiang
Luo, Zhenzhen
Zhao, Yanan
Zhu, Yuanhua
Yang, Hualin
Zhou, Yu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a dual-model immunoassay for detecting Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) was developed based on 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) and carbon dots (CDs). Under the catalysis of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the o-phthalylenediamine (OPD) was oxidized to DAP which had a yellow color and intense fluorescence. The color changes form colorless to yellow was used to design absorbance model immunoassay. Meanwhile, the absorption spectrum of DAP overlapped with the emission spectrum of CDs which caused the fluorescence of CDs to be quenched. The fluorescence changes of DAP and CDs were used to develop ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay. The dual-model immunoassay showed excellent sensitivity with the limits of detection (LODs) of 0.013 ng/mL for fluorescence mode and 0.062 ng/mL for absorbance mode. Meanwhile, both models exhibited great selectivity for AFB1. Additionally, the recovery rates suggested the proposed dual-model immunoassay had great potential in actual samples detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aflatoxin B1
Dual-model
Immunoassay
Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance strategy for AFB1
Principle of the proposed dual-model immunoassay
Analysis of AFB1 in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 32272411 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (No. 2023AFB667 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. (A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and photographs of CDs under natural light at 365 nm UV (inset). (B) TEM images of CDs. (C) FT-IR spectrum. (D)XRD pattern. (E) XPS full spectrum. High resolution XPS spectra of (F) C1s. (G) N1s. (H) O1s.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of DAP; Inset: the photographs of DAP under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (B) Fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs, and the UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD and DAP. (C) Fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs and DAP. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, CDs + OPD and CDs + OPD + HRP + H2O2; Inset: the corresponding fluorescence photographs under UV light.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and DAP with different concentrations of HRP-IgG. (B) The variations of F560/F460 with different concentrations of HRP-IgG. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of DAP with different concentrations of HRP-IgG; Inset was the photographs of DAP under natural light. (D) The variations of absorbance intensity with different concentrations of HRP-IgG.

(A) The variations of F560/F460 with different concentrations of AFB1. (B) The variations of absorbance with different concentrations of AFB1. The selectivity of dual-model immunoassay in (C) ratiometric fluorescence model and (D) absorbance model. The concentration of AFB1 was 1.25 ng/mL, the concentration of other mycotoxins was 100 times that of AFB1 (125 ng/mL.
Schematic diagram of the dual-model immunoassay for AFB1 detection.
Recovery of AFB1 from real samples using the new dual-model immunoassay.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Coefficient of variation = standard deviation/mean value × 100 %.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Recovery = measured AFB1 concentration/added AFB1 concentration × 100 %.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance dual-model immunoassay based on 2,3-diaminophenazine and carbon dots for detecting Aflatoxin B1

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Yangtze University, 266 Jingmi Road, Jingzhou, Hubei 434025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Yangtze University, 266 Jingmi Road, Jingzhou, Hubei 434025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Animal Science and Technology, Yangtze University, 266 Jingmi Road, Jingzhou, Hubei 434025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Animal Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Animal Science and Technology, Yangtze University, 266 Jingmi Road, Jingzhou, Hubei 434025, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Life Science, Yangtze University, 266 Jingmi Road, Jingzhou, Hubei 434025, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a dual-model immunoassay for detecting Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) was developed based on 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) and carbon dots (CDs). Under the catalysis of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the o-phthalylenediamine (OPD) was oxidized to DAP which had a yellow color and intense fluorescence. The color changes form colorless to yellow was used to design absorbance model immunoassay. Meanwhile, the absorption spectrum of DAP overlapped with the emission spectrum of CDs which caused the fluorescence of CDs to be quenched. The fluorescence changes of DAP and CDs were used to develop ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay. The dual-model immunoassay showed excellent sensitivity with the limits of detection (LODs) of 0.013 ng/mL for fluorescence mode and 0.062 ng/mL for absorbance mode. Meanwhile, both models exhibited great selectivity for AFB1. Additionally, the recovery rates suggested the proposed dual-model immunoassay had great potential in actual samples detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxin was secondary metabolites generated by Aspergillus flavus with extremely harmful (Mahato, Lee, Kamle, Devi, Dewangan, Kumar, et al., 2019). It was widely found in food products, feed ingredients, agriculture products and various organic substances, more than ten types had been isolated and identified (Nazhand, Durazzo, Lucarini, Souto, & Santini, 2020). Among of them, aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) was the most toxic (Xue, Zhang, Yu, Zhang, Wang, Wan, et al., 2019), which resulted in numerous food and agricultural products pollution, and caused massive losses to human production and healthy life. Due to mutagenicity (Al-Ayoubi, Alonso-Jauregui, Azqueta, Vignard, Mirey, Rocher, et al., 2023; Minko, Kellum, Stone, & Lloyd, 2023), teratogenicity (Dey & Kang, 2020) and carcinogenicity (Cao, Yu, Yang, & Cao, 2022; Y. Liu, Liu, Li, Cui, Yun, Zhang, et al., 2022), AFB1 was classified as a Group I carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) (Ostry, Malir, Toman, & Grosse, 2016). The European Union (EU) had established a maximum permissible concentration of AFB1 for 2 μg/kg in cereals and cereal products (Yang, Yin, Li, Wang, & Dong, 2020). Thus, it was urgently needed a sensitive and specific method for detecting AFB1.

In recent years, many technologies had been developed for the detection of AFB1, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (AlFaris, Altamimi, Alothman, Al Qahtani, Wabaidur, Ghfar, et al., 2020; Algammal, Elsayed, Hashem, Ramadan, Sheraba, El-Diasty, et al., 2021), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Chen, Ye, Li, Wu, Liu, Xuan, et al., 2022), thin-layer chromatography (TLC) (Qu, Jia, Liu, Wang, Duan, Yang, et al., 2018) and surface enhanced raman scattering (SERS) (Jiao, Ahmad, Zhu, Hassan, Wang, Rong, et al., 2021; Zhang, Jiang, Li, Yuan, & Yang, 2023). Despite their superior performance, these methods still faced challenges such as complex operations, intricate sample preparation, and expensive instrumentation, which limited their practical applicability for large-scale sample screening (Jia, Zhou, Liu, Li, & Yu, 2019; Zhan, Hu, Li, Huang, & Xiong, 2021). For solving these problems, immunoassay technologies had attracted much attention. Among immunoassay technologies, the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) was recognized as one of the most excellent tools due to their simplicity, economy and high throughput (Xu, Long, Chen, Chen, & Cheng, 2021; Yan, Zhu, Li, He, Yang, & Liu, 2022). Nevertheless, the conventional ELISA that was triggered by horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-catalyzed chromogenic substrate still exhibited some drawbacks, such as limited sensitivity and false positive results (Yildirim-Tirgil, 2023; Yuan, Liu, Yin, & Xu, 2019). Consequently, it was imperative to develop novel methods to enhance sensitivity and accuracy. Integrating functional nanomaterials with conventional detection techniques was an effective way (X. Wang, Niessner, Tang, & Knopp, 2016).

Carbon dots (CDs) were promising optical zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials (Barman & Patra, 2018). CDs offered multiple advantages over conventional fluorescent materials, such as high-water solubility, low cost, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility (Dai, Chen, & Hu, 2023; M. L. Liu, Chen, Li, & Huang, 2019; Yusuf, Atulbhai, Swapna, Malek, & Kailasa, 2022). Based on CDs fluorescent probes, fluorescence quenching, fluorescence amplification, and off/on fluorescence switching methods were developed to detect target (Dai & Hu, 2022; Song, Liu, Zhang, Liu, Meng, Yin, et al., 2018; Wang, Li, Lu, Fan, He, Huang, et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the CDs-based fluorescence immunoassays were also designed (Li, Yan, Lu, & Su, 2018). However, most of the methods were single signal output mode, which was not conducive to the removal of false positive results.

In this study, the o-phthalylenediamine (OPD) was employed as the substrate of the HRP. The OPD can be oxidized to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with yellow color and intense fluorescence. Due to the absorption peak of DAP overlapped with the emission peak of CDs, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by DAP. Based on the fluorescence pairs of DAP and CDs, a ratiometric fluorescence reporting signal immunoassay can be established. Meanwhile, as OPD changed to DAP, the color changed from colorless to yellow. Absorbance and color can be used as another output signals mode. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance dual-model immunoassay was proposed based on DAP and CDs for detecting AFB1.

Mycotoxins including AFB1, deoxynivalenol (DON), ochratoxin A (OTA), T-2 toxin (T-2) and zearalenone (ZEN) were supplied by Qingdao Pribolab Biotechnology Co, Ltd (Qingdao, China). Urea and β-d-glucose was attained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine (OPD) was acquired from Sigma-Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). AFB1 conjugated BSA (AFB1-BSA), mouse anti-AFB1 monoclonal antibody (McAb) and HRP-labelled goat anti-mouse IgG (HRP-IgG) were acquired from Dalian Fine Inspection Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Dalian, China). Corn samples were got from local supermarket (Jingzhou, Hubei province, China). Fluorescence, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance were recorded on SpectraMax® iD3 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader (USA).The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from HT-7800 (Japan).

CDs were prepared following the reported method (R Thara, Korah, Mathew, John, & Mathew, 2023). Firstly, urea (2 g) and β-d-glucose (1 g) were mixed in deionized water (20 mL). Then, the mixture was microwaved about 20 min. During this process, all the water was evaporated, and the brown solid was obtained which were the N-doped carbon dots. The CDs were purified by sonicating and filtering in deionized water (50 mL). Finally, the CDs were kept at 4 °C.

The following steps were taken to study the HRP activity. Firstly, 20 μL 6 mM OPD and 20 μL 10 mM H2O2 were mixed with 30 μL CDs. Then, different concentrations of HRP (from 0 to 60 ng/mL) were added into aforesaid mixture. Later, the fluorescence emission spectra and absorption spectra were collected. The excitation wavelength was 380 nm, and the emission wavelength was from 420 nm to 750 nm. The absorbance wavelength was from 360 nm to 600 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance strategy for AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: First, 100 μL 0.6 μg/mL AFB1-BSA was coated in the 96-well plate and incubated for 1.5 h at 37 °C. All of the following reactions were incubated at 37 °C. After three washes with phosphate tween buffer (PBST), 100 μL 5 % skim milk was added for blocking. After repeating the above procedures for washing, 50 μL AFB1 (0.25–5 ng/ mL) and 50 μL 1 μg/mL of McAb were incubated for 60 min. After washing, 100 μL 0.8 μg/mL HRP-IgG was added and incubated for 60 min. Afterwards, washing again, 30 μL CDs, 20 μL 6 mM OPD and 20 μL 10 mM H2O2 were mixed into 130 μL phosphate buffer (PBS) and incubated for 30 min. At last, the fluorescence and absorbance were collected.

The recoveries of AFB1 from actual samples were examined for assessing the accuracy of the proposed dual-model method (Xiao, Zhang, Ma, Fang, Yang, & Zhou, 2022). The steps were as follow. First, AFB1 was added to the milled corn flour sample and stored overnight at room temperature. The final concentrations of AFB1 were 0, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6 ng/mL. Then, samples were added 70 % methanol-aqueous solution, sonicated for 10 min, and centrifuged at 4000 g for 10 min. Afterwards, the supernatant was diluted 20 times with PBS buffer, and the samples were analyzed according to the dual-model immunoassay. At last, the recoveries and the coefficient of variation (CV) were calculated. The recoveries = measured AFB1 concentration/added AFB1 concentration × 100 %. The CV = standard deviation/mean value × 100 %.

The structural and optical properties of CDs were characterized by fluorescence spectrum and TEM. The CDs solution was almost colorless under natural light, and had a strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Inset of Fig. 1
A). The fluorescence spectra revealed the CDs had a fluorescence excitation peak at 360 nm and fluorescence emission peak at 460 nm, and the UV–vis absorption spectrum demonstrated that CDs had two absorption peaks at 220 nm and 270 nm (Fig. 1 A). The TEM image showed CDs was uniformly distributed and had a quasi-spherical structure (Fig. 1 B). The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated to be 9.1 % by relative fluorescence method with quinine sulfate as the reference (QY = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4)(R Thara, Korah, Mathew, John, & Mathew, 2023). The pattern of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) showed a broad bands at 3400 cm−1 which corresponded to the stretching vibration of OH and NH bonds. The peak of 1610 cm−1 corresponded to the sp2-hybridized carbon (CC). The band at 1320 cm−1 was attributed to CN band (Fig. 1 C). The pattern of X-Ray diffraction (XRD) showed that the peak was 260 in 2θ diffraction which corresponded to the (002) plane of carbon with some disordered carbon atoms (Fig. 1 D). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicated the main components of CDs were C, N, and O (Fig. 1 E). The high resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s indicated the CDs were made up of CC/CC bonds, CNC/ CN bonds and CO/ CO bonds which was consistent with the FT-IR results (Fig. 1 F-H) (Dai, Chen, & Hu, 2023; R Thara, Korah, Mathew, John, & Mathew, 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the proposed dual-model immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the dual-model immunoassay was shown in Scheme 1 . First, the AFB1-BSA was coated on the 96-well plate, and the AFB1-BSA can compete with AFB1 for binding McAb to form an AFB1-BSA@McAb. After addition of HRP-IgG, the AFB1-BSA@McAb@HRP-IgG complex was formed which was negatively correlated with the concentration of AFB1 due to the competitive immunoreaction. In the presence of H2O2, the complex can oxidize OPD to DAP. The DAP emitted yellow color and intense fluorescence at 560 nm. Meanwhile, the DAP can quench the fluorescence of CDs (the peak fluorescence was 460 nm) because the absorption spectrum of DAP was overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs. In fluorescence model, the 560 nm fluorescence of DAP increased and the 460 nm fluorescence of CDs decreased as the decreased concentration of AFB1. Meanwhile, the yellow color of DAP was observed for color model. Thus, the ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance dual-model immunoassay can be developed for detecting AFB1.

At first, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of DAP were measured. The fluorescence emission peak of DAP was 560 nm, whereas the maximum excitation wavelength was 420 nm (Fig. 2
A). The DAP solution was yellow under natural light and emitted a strong yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Inset of Fig. 2 A). According to the mechanism of the dual-model immunoassay, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by DAP was a critical factor. As the result, DAP had an absorption peak at 420 nm that partially overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (the red and blue line in Fig. 2 B). Thus, DAP can quench the fluorescence of CDs. However, the OPD had no absorption and no quenching function for CDs (gray line of Fig. 2 B). To further investigate the sensing mechanism, fluorescence lifetime of CDs in the presence and absence of DAP (quencher) were measured as 4.49 ns and 4.74 ns, respectively (
Fig. S1
). Because the two lifetimes were nearly the same, the inner filter effect was responsible for the fluorescence quenching(Dai & Hu, 2022).

Theoretically, since the excitation peaks of CD (360 nm) and DAP (420 nm) were different, the emission spectra of CD and DAP needed to be measured, separately. To make the measurements easier and faster, the fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs and DAP were scanned. Then, the intersection point of the two excitation wavelengths (380 nm) was chosen as the common excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 C). Under 380 nm excitation wavelength, the fluorescence changes of CDs and DAP were further verified (Fig. 2 D). The CDs still showed a high fluorescence at 460 nm (black line of Fig. 2 D). When the OPD was added, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was unaffected (red line of Fig. 2 D). When the HRP and H2O2 were added, the DAP was generated. The new fluorescence excitation peak at 560 nm was observed, and the fluorescence of CDs was quenched (blue line of Fig. 2 D). Meanwhile, the fluorescence photographs also showed the same results (Inset of Fig. 2 D). Thus, the signal output of the dual-model immunoassay was feasible.

Because the immune recognition for AFB1 and the signal output were connected by HRP-IgG, the changes of fluorescence and absorbance with different concentrations of HRP-IgG were investigated firstly. With the HRP-IgG concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm (F460) gradually decreased, and the fluorescence intensity at 560 nm (F560) gradually increased (Fig. 3
A). Subsequently, the F560/F460 was employed for quantitative analysis (Fig. 3 B). The results showed the F560/F460 and the concentration of HRP-IgG were linearly correlated in the range of 10–40 ng/mL (Inset of Fig. 3 B). The linear equation was Y = 7.209X − 6.376 (R2 = 0.988), where the Y was F560/F460 and X was the logarithm of the HRP-IgG concentration. For absorbance model, with the concentrations of HRP-IgG increased, the absorption peak at 420 nm was gradually increased (Fig. 3 C). The corresponding color changes from colorless to yellow can also be directly observed with the naked eye (Inset of Fig. 3 C). The changes of absorbance at 420 nm with different HRP-IgG was researched by curve equation fitting (Fig. 3 D). A great linear relationship between absorbance and the logarithm of the HRP-IgG concentration was obtained in the range of 7.5–30 ng/mL (Inset of Fig. 3 D). The standard linear equation was Y = 1.469X − 1.028, (R2 = 0.982), where the Y was absorbance intensity and X was the logarithm of the HRP-IgG concentration. The results showed that the ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance intensity can be used to sense the change of HRP concentration, and also indicated that the HRP-IgG can be used to connect target AFB1 and dual-model signal.

In order to develop the most efficient dual-model immunoassay, the concentrations of H2O2, OPD and HRP-IgG, the incubation time of HRP-IgG with OPD were optimized. As the concentrations of H2O2, OPD and HRP-IgG increased the signals of F560/F460 and absorbance increased. When the concentrations of H2O2, OPD and HRP-IgG reached 1 mM, 0.6 mM, and 0.8 μg/mL, respectively, the F560/F460 and absorbance values no longer increased and tended to saturation (
Fig. S2
,
Fig. S3
,
Fig. S4
). Thus, the 1 mM H2O2, 0.6 mM OPD, and 0.8 μg/mL HRP-IgG were chosen as the optimum working concentration. For the incubation time of HRP-IgG with OPD, F560/F460 and absorbance values increased gradually before 30 min (
Fig. S5
). Then the values of F560/F460 and absorbance would no longer increase. Thus, 30 min was chosen. Finally, the concentration of AFB1-BSA (0.6 μg/mL) and McAb (1 μg/mL) were selected from the checkerboard method ( Table S1, S2 ) where the ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance intensity had greatest changes under the minimum dose of AFB1-BSA and McAb.

The sensitivity and linear detection range of the dual-model immunoassay were studied by establishing a standard curve under optimized experimental conditions. With different concentration of AFB1, the changes of F560/F460 were observed for ratiometric fluorescence model ( Fig. 4
A). In the concentration of 0.75–1.80 ng/mL, the AFB1 and F560/F460 showed a good linear relationship (Inset of Fig. 4 A). The linear equation was Y = − 3.233X + 6.129 (R2 = 0.991), where the Y was value of F560/F460, and the X was the concentration of AFB1. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.013 ng/mL based on 3σ/Slope (Ma, Zhang, Xiao, Fang, Zhang, Yang, et al., 2023). The changes of absorbance intensity were also researched for absorbance model ( Fig. 4 B). The absorbance and the concentration of AFB1 fitted a linear equation in the range of 0.75–1.80 ng/mL (Inset of Fig. 4 B). The linear equation was Y = − 0.694X + 1.391 (R2 = 0.991), where the Y was value of absorbance intensity, and the X was the concentration of AFB1. The LOD was estimated to be 0.062 ng/mL in absorbance model. Since the linear ranges of the two modes were consistent (0.75–1.80 ng/mL), the detection results of the proposed dual-model immunoassay can be mutually verified to improve the accuracy. Besides, the new dual-model immunoassay was compared with the traditional ic-ELISA with the same antibody ( Fig. S6 ). The LOD of fluorescence model (0.013 ng/mL) was about 20-fold lower than that of traditional ic-ELISA (0.27 ng/mL). And LOD of the absorbance model (0.062 ng/mL) was more than 4 times lower than traditional methods (0.27 ng/mL). Furthermore, the linear ranges of the dual-model immunoassay (0.75–1.80 ng/mL) was larger than the traditional methods (0.29–0.61 ng/mL). Besides, this new immunoassay also was compared with reported fluorescence and absorbance immunoassay ( Table S3 ). The results showed our immunoassay had higher sensitivity with lower LOD. And the dual-model of our immunoassay also had advantage in detection accuracy compared with the single mode methods.

In order to analyze the selectivity of the immunoassay, the responses of the new method to different mycotoxins were investigated including AFB1, OTA, ZEN, DON and T-2. Both ratiometric fluorescence model and absorbance model showed that only AFB1 can cause significant signal changes, while the other mycotoxins were at a blank level even though the concentration was 100 times greater than AFB1 ( Fig. 4 C and D). The results indicated the dual-model immunoassay had good selectivity for detecting AFB1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AFB1 in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a novel ratiometric fluorescence and absorbance dual-model immunoassay was developed based on the color of DAP and the fluorescence of DAP/CDs. The yellow color of DAP can be directly observed by the naked eye for semi-quantitative detection, and also can be measured by instruments for quantitative detection. The fluorescence changes of DAP/CDs were used to achieve ratiometric fluorescence detection which can improve the accuracy of the results. By finding the intersection of CDs and DAP excitation wavelengths, the co-excitation of CDs and DAP fluorescence can be achieved which made the fluorescence measurement more convenient. Besides, the accuracy and reliability of the method were greatly improved by the mutual verification of two modes. The synthesis of CDs did not require complex steps and expensive materials. In addition, the dual-model immunoassay showed satisfactory selectivity for AFB1 and great application potential in real sample detection.

Die Xu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Junxiang Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zhenzhen Luo: Data curation, Visualization. Yanan Zhao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yuanhua Zhu: Data curation. Hualin Yang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yu Zhou: Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 32272411) and the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (No. 2023AFB667).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.138125.
